speaker: interviewee
text: [TONE] [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: We can't photograph it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't want to photograph it. Just for you. OK. For your information. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: December 7th, 1995. The survivor is Sam Fasenfest. He was born Fasenfest-- his last name. I'm Joyce Tapper, the interviewer, in San Mateo, California. We're in the United States of America. We'll be speaking English. I'm Joyce Tapper. I'm the interviewer today with Mr. Sam Fasenfest. We're in San Mateo, California. We're speaking English. It's December 7, 1995. Please begin, Mr. Fasenfest.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sam Joseph Fasenfest. I was born in Brezeziny, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: On May the 17th, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shlomo. Shlomo. In Polish, it would be Shlamach. But they usually called me Shlomo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about what life was like in this small-- was this a small town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a-- it was a small town, yeah. It was, I would think, probably around 20,000 people. My father was a tailor. And we were not very-- we were comfortable. We were not rich, but we were comfortable. And we lived there, until I was about five, six. I started cheder. And we went to-- I went to school then at seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was the cheder from your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably a few blocks. Maybe five, six blocks. And I went to cheder for about two or three years, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you like it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I liked it very much. You know, it's just-- probably we went to cheder, you know, and we learned the Jewishness, you know, all about the Jewish life, you know. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go walk there by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I have-- I had one brother, and my brother was four years older. He was born on December the 31st, 1919. And we lived in Brezeziny till-- oh, probably until I was eight, nine years old. Then we moved to a still a smaller place, called Jezow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, before you tell us about Jezow, tell us a little bit more about Brzeziny.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Brzeziny--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother-- actually, in Brzeziny, my mother did not work. Just, you know, she was a housewife. You know? And she kept house--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: --for us. Miriam. Miriam. We called her Manya. In Polish, it was Manya. But her name, her Jewish name, was Miriam. And she kept the household for us, you know? And I and my brother went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the household like? Was it an apartment or a house? Describe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it wasn't an apartment. It was a, a large room. Well, we called it, you know, an apartment. But actually it was a one-room apartment. It was a large room. And as I mentioned, we were not too well off. We were just comfortably at, you know, the way as Jews lived in Poland. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have enough to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. But not, not maybe specialities. But we were, we were comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: You all slept in the same room?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all slept in the same room, yes. And we all-- my father also did the work in the same room. But it was a large room. But this is how, how most of the people lived in Poland, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- actually we played some soccer, you know, as a child. You know, we played soccer. But it wasn't too much time. Because either when we were off school, we went back to cheder. You know, the school was usually four-- four-hour school, from the morning till about noontime. And at noontime, you know, we came home, maybe had something to eat, you know. And we went back in the afternoons to the cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this school a school where there were the local children? Were there Gentile children in this school as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was-- it was a, it was a local school. But it was-- although it was a small town, it was strictly-- the school was a grade school, but it was strictly a Jewish school for Jewish boys and girls. You know? The reason probably was that it would be too much friction if there would be Jewish boys with the Gentile boys. That's what I think, that it would be too much friction. That's the reason why there was a strictly Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there much interaction between you and the Gentile children outside school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really. If there was interaction, it was just, you know, teasing. You know, more teasing than, than pleasantness and, and friendship.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say the teasing was anti-Semitic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably so. Because you have to realize that Poland was-- was and still is an anti-Semitic country. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any incidents from that early part of your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember one incident as-- if I may say, in, from Jezow. When we moved from Brzeziny, we moved to Jezow. Jezow was still a smaller city. It was almost a village. And in that village, I went-- about a year or a year and a half we lived there. We went to school with Gentile children, because there were not enough Jewish children to go to-- to have a Jewish school. And it was always teasing and fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one incident-- I remember that the, the teacher made always fun of the Jewish boys. He always made fun. You know, like if he would call out something, you know, he would not call out the real name. But he would call, hey, Junge! What do you think about it? And this was more an insult. You know? It was insulting. However, we were too small, you know, to realize, and we were-- we took it just for granted that that's it. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And let's say for instance we played soccer. You know, most of the time, we would be the ones who were watching it, because they wouldn't let us play. They wouldn't-- and you have to remember that this was when I was seven, eight, nine years old. You know, it was, it was bad. But this was-- we were, we were brought up like this. And we didn't think anything about it. It was just for us it was normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now why did your family move to the second place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother was also from-- when she was still single, and her mother were making wigs. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they make wigs for Jewish woman who didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: They made-- only. Only for Jewish women. You know, the wigs like here, today, people wear wigs for beauty or for change of things. At that time, Jewish people-- Jewish women-- wore wigs only to go to the synagogue, you know? And because, you know, that the, the Jewish women did not go without a head cover. And you know, if they went to, to the synagogue, they would have to have either a, either a handkerchief, a neckerchief, or, or most of them had a sheit-- a wig, which we called a sheitel. And my mother used to make the sheitels. And when we moved to Jezow, my mother was almost the sole house supplier of, of many of them. And she did make sheitels for the, for the Jewish women there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did she work on these wigs?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home. As a matter of fact, when I was small-- well, at that time, you know, she showed us how to. And she said if we want to-- me and my brother-- sometimes we even helped out, you know, a little thing, how to make. I know my mother, she had several wooden heads, you know? And she put on the gauze on it, you know? And she taught us how to, how to thread, you know, the hair and make the sheitels.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did she-- where did she get the hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe she bought them in Lodz. Lodz is-- was-- Lodz is the largest-- the second largest town in Poland. And this was about, I would say, probably about 30, 40 miles from Jezow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you like helping out with this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I did not know if I like it. I probably had to because my mother say, you better do it or else. And we did it. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they use real hair from women that sold their hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably. At that time, I don't think so that the-- that it was everything modern as today, as they use artificial hair. Probably it was always real hair. Where they come from, I don't know. I have no idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I know that my mother bought them in Lodz. And you know, she bought them several shades, you know, for people. You know, they're dark and darker and, or lighter, you know? And she-- Did the ladies come to your house to try these on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe how that was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, I don't know much about it. But they came and, you know, and they gave my mother the order. My mother took, took the sizing of the head, and the color, everything, and how. And my mother then made it, and then, then it was-- when she finished, they came and picked them up. And before the Jewish holidays, like Yom Kippur and Rosh Hashanah, my mother was real busy. Because not only did she have to deliver the wigs, but people who had the wigs came and asked her, you know, to make, make the [INAUDIBLE] and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's a [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- you know, to make the hair curly. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: To wash it. To clean it and then fix it again?

speaker: interviewee
text: To clean it and fix it again, and cut it and prepare it. And I know that for the two, three days before the holidays, my mother was real busy, you know? And she made quite, quite, at that time, quite a good money in order to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say she did-- brought in more money than your father was able to.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, yes. At that time. And after about two years, my parents decided that they will move to Lodz. As I mentioned before, Lodz was the second largest city. It's a, it's a textile manufacturing city. And my father figured that in Lodz, in a bigger city, he would be able to find work and, and make a better living. And we moved to Lodz, I believe, in 1933 when I was nine or 10 years old. I remember 1933, because at that year, the Marshal of Poland, Pilsudski, he died in that year. And this-- at that time we were already in Lodz. So I know for sure that in 1933 we were in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the living conditions in Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, again, we lived in an apartment building. And again, I say apartment-- it was strictly one room. It was a large room. But it was an apartment building. We lived on the third floor. And it was an apartment building. Roughly probably about 20 or 25 one-room apartments-- the whole house. You know, the whole building had one-room apartments. And we lived on the third floor. It was a large room, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the four of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The four of us lived in that room, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you cook? They, we had the-- everything in that room. There was a stove-- well, a stove, a range, you know, that we made there. Well, we made-- it was from bricks. It was not-- it was built in a brick, brick range, you know? And we lived there. And as I say, we were used to one-room living. And we were quite comfortable. Although we lived there, and we worked there, and we slipt there, and we slept there. You know, of course we slept two people in a bed. We had two beds. Were you with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my brother, you know, and my parents in the, in the other bed. And my brother-- at that time, my brother finished school. He was already 14. And my brother started to help out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he helped out-- my father was a tailor, and he helped out. At that time, my parents decided that they will manufacture clothing. And we will sell it on the market. Well, market, I mean-- in Lodz, there was a street market like here in the United States we have fairs. But we have only here fairs twice a year or once a year. Over there, the fair was twice a week. Every Tuesday and Friday we had a fair. And we worked in the, at home. We manufactured the clothing. And Tuesday and Friday, we went out to the market, to the fair, and we sold it. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do? What was your contribution?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my contribution was that we had to carry out, you know, all the clothing and, you know, the tables, you know, and the things to the, to the market. And we sell--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to sort of set up a booth, like--

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- well, it was not a booth. It was just something like a table. And everything was on the table. And people came, you know, and tried it out. And, and it was quite comfortable. It was-- my father did quite well with that. Well, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of clothing did you make?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of it was for children-- coats, children coats and lady coats. Because my father was what's called a lady garment maker. You know? And we were quite, quite successful, as I say, at that one. At certain-- at the later years, in '37 and '38, we were quite-- so successful that we had already two people-- two and sometimes even three people-- in the season helping us out making the clothing. Which it was-- you know, it was already four or five people made it, so it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: All in the same room?

speaker: interviewee
text: All in the same room. We never, we never left the room. But as I say, as I said, it was quite a large room. But we, we were able to get by with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as a matter of fact, sometimes, you know, during the week, some people came even during the week up. Because, you know, they came on the fair, you know, and we didn't have the same, the same size or the, the color. And we said, why don't you come over? We'll have it in two, three days. And some people came up even during the week. You know? And we were-- Did you go to school in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I finished--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I finished in Lodz-- I went to school in Lodz-- grammar school. I, as well as my brother, finished only grammar school. In grammar school in Poland, you started at 7 and you went 7 years. From 7 to 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this a mixed school of Gentiles and Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. In Lodz, we were mixed boys and girls, but strictly Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the name. But I remember the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called Mlinarska. Mlinarska-- which is more like Mill Street in Poland-- Mlinarska two, Mill Street two. And there I went to school, and there I graduated.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish community like in Lodz for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was quite-- the Jewish was a large community. Lodz was the second largest city, and it had a population roughly probably of about a half a million or more. And I think just about more than half-- about 250,000 to 300,000 people in Lodz were Jews. So it was quite a large--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: --a large Jewish-- yes we went to-- I never meant to a cheder there, but we went to the synagogue. You know, the synagogue was on [POLISH] Street. You know, it was, it was called the Old City synagogue. It was a lovely synagogue. And we went there definitely on the high holidays and also on some Friday nights or Saturdays. But not regularly, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you say your family was observant? Or how would you describe your family life Jewishly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were-- if-- we ate kosher, and we had separately milchig and fleishig you know? But observant that we should-- that my father would pray three times a day, probably not. You know? But we definitely had a kosher, a kosher household. We would never buy meat or anything from, from a Gentile butcher, or we would never mix meat and milk together. No. We had separate dishes. But we were not, we were not really religious. We were just more, I would say, progressive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your mother wear a wig?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My mother made wigs, but she never wore a wig. This is the funny part about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you could see her hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually we didn't do much for fun. When I graduated from school, I started at home, and I worked already at home. I helped already on sewing. And although maybe in the later years, probably it helped me a little bit that I had an idea of sewing. But I was no tailor. But after all, I was a boy-- 14 years old, you know. So, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have anti-Semitic problems?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably in general, yes. But personally that I can remember, I cannot remember. But I know that usually, we lived in a-- on the street and about two, three blocks from us were, was a, was a church. And I remember that on Sundays when the church was out, you know, we were advised not to be on the street. Because you know-- not officially, but you know, some-- you know, either they would kick you or, or spit on you or hit you. So but-- it was just-- we knew about it, and we didn't, we didn't go out at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever get kicked, spit at, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not, not really. No. I cannot say that I had any personal experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when was your first sign that trouble was brewing? When did you first began to worry about war coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as a matter of fact, we were not very well informed. However, the previous year, in 1938, some German Jews came from Germany to Lodz. They came. And we heard about them. And we knew that the Jewish community helped them out, helped them resettle. But they were-- not in a great amount came to Lodz. But there came some people. We knew about it. And this was in '38. And the Germans, you know they said that they want Danzig. You know, Danzig is a city way up north, you know, that it was actually a free, a free city. Which means neither the Germans governed it nor the Poles government. It was-- it's a port. And most of the people in Danzig were Germans. And the Germans said that Danzig belongs to us and we want it back. But it was not anything officially worrisome, until--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not. But my brother was-- my brother belonged to the Gordonia. It was, it was a Jewish, a Jewish Zionist organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did you think about going to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I didn't even think. We were not able to-- we were not that well off, and we were not thinking about it. No. We were not. In 1939, when the war broke out-- the war broke out on September the first, the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened that day? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly that day. But I know that they said that the Germans crossed the border. And all I know is that seven days after the war broke out, around the 7th or the 8th, the Germans were already in Lodz. It didn't take--

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the first thing you saw.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the very first thing what we saw, we saw them on the motorcycles. You know, they came in, you know, the first, the, the first part, or the first of them-- the First Battalion of them. They came on motorcycles, you know, with, with all the weapons and everything. And they marched through-- in Lodz, there was not much, not much people that they did confront them. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no bombing before they came?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- not really. In Lodz, actually not. All the bombing we heard, it was in, in Warsaw. That they bombed Warsaw. But in Lodz, I cannot remember that there was any bombing. Maybe they flew over. Maybe they dropped a bomb or two. But I cannot remember but that it should be a real bombing definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or any fear of bombing where you had to go out in the night?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No, no, no. However, after several days they were in, they instituted what they called a sperre, that after 6 o'clock in the evening, no one is allowed to go on the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: A curfew.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it was a curfew, something like a curfew, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you react when you saw the Germans and when you knew this? What was the family talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: At, at the first, we did not expect that they will be so militantly, and so anti-Semitic and so, so it-- that it will be so bad for the Jews. We didn't expect-- maybe we were, we were too ignorant to know about it. The thing that I remember first-- there was one incident. As I said before, we were, we sold our clothing on the market, on the fair, on Fridays. And it was two or, about two or three weeks after the Germans arrived, and things. My father went out in the morning, the early morning, with the-- to put the table on it, you know, to take his, his place and things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came back white, all completely white, you know? And my mother asked him, what happened? Don't ask. Don't ask. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said that on the marketplace, they had hanged seven people. And they were just on the view. If they were Jews or Gentiles, I don't know. I don't remember. But my father said that there were seven people hanging on the thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as a matter of fact, they hanged the whole day. I would think that it was more or less a point that the Germans wanted to make that the people should be afraid of them. You know? And they put it up with a little shill that they caught them for spying, or that they were out too late on the curfew, or whatever. And I know that they hung there the whole day on that Friday. Were these men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were men. And they were in the--

words.text: men. And they were in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did see them. I had a glance of them. Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me-- tell me what that was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was terrible. It was-- they were hanging, you know, on a, on a big rack. You know, it was quite-- it wasn't high, but it was on a big rack, you know? And one hanging one next to the other one. Something to hang-- a rack like here you would hang clothing on. You know? It was on full view, and they were hanging there the whole day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you go about looking at this? You were how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I was there 15-- this was 16 years. I was 16 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: You left the house, and what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't leave the house. We still sold the clothing there on the market. We-- it didn't affect. It was just on the other side of the thing. And we just pretended that, that, that nothing happened. What can you do? We had to, to go on making a living. This is, this is how we made our living.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that day you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: That day we realized, you know, that we should be afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, how I felt. I felt bad. How can you feel? This was nothing to, nothing to be joyful about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, next, next came on December. On December, we were informed that Lodz will be incorporated into Germany-- that they figured that Lodz-- Lodz is actually in Western, West Poland. And they wanted to take Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you informed about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, rumors. Rumors that they will incorporate-- that they are gonna take over Lodz. And Lodz will be under their protection and so. And on December, on the last, on the latest, latest part of December-- the last week, or the last two weeks-- one afternoon-- yeah. Let me come back before. In the beginning of December, my parents decided that my brother should go to Warsaw. Warsaw, it was to the east, closer to the Russian border.

speaker: interviewee
text: And why did they decide this? Well, they decided that he should go to Warsaw, and in Warsaw, maybe because it's not gonna-- Lodz is not-- Warsaw is not going to be incorporated into Germany, maybe life will be easier there. My brother decided, and we packed everything, and that he will go.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my brother had a, a girlfriend. And at that time, the parent of my brother's girlfriend said, yes, they can go together. My brother was going together with the girl. But you have to marry before you go. It was a different life. It wasn't like, like it is now. They give-- will give the blessing that the girl should go with my brother to Warsaw, but they have to marry first.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Manya, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Goldberg. And they lived also in Lodz. And they went to, to a rabbi. They went to a rabbi, and they got married on the latest part of December.
